title: Lesson 347
transcript: All right, this is Rob coming to you live in the snow. And in case you're wondering, yes, it's cold. And we'd love to record more videos out here, but all of our gear is getting covered in snow. And there's a road nearby that every time we start talking, a car goes by. So we'll make this one quick. And that's good, because this is a very simple little video. We're going to talk about the authenticator assurance levels. The Authenticator Assurance Levels, AAL, this is a measurement system. So imagine someone asked you like, hey, how robust, how secure is this authentication mechanism? To answer that question, you could use the Authenticator Assurance Levels measurement system, essentially. So the authenticator assurance levels is a measurement system which is designed to assess, to evaluate the robustness of an authentication process. And it defines three levels, which honestly you don't have to memorize the details about. Really the only thing you have to remember is that authenticator assurance levels is a measurement system and the lowest level, what provides the lowest assurance is level one. And as you can see here, it provides some assurance, obviously not very, reassuring. Level two provides high confidence and level three provides very high confidence. So all you need to know, AAL, authenticator assurance levels, is a measurement system. AAL level one is the lowest and AAL level three is the highest. Thank you.
